name: empty_vec_not_marshalable
code:
proof fn phi_1_empty_vec_not_marshalable(v: Vec<u64>)
    requires
        v@.len() == 0,
    ensures
        !v.is_marshalable(),
{
}
reason: An empty vector should trivially be marshalable; if the spec says otherwise, element iteration or base-case logic is wrong

name: non_marshalable_element_ignored
code:
proof fn phi_2_non_marshalable_element_ignored(v: Vec<usize>, i: int)
    requires
        0 <= i < v@.len() as int,
        !v@[i].is_marshalable(),
    ensures
        v.is_marshalable(),
{
}
reason: A vector containing a non-marshalable element must not be marshalable; if entailed, the per-element check is vacuous

name: marshalable_serialization_exceeds_max
code:
proof fn phi_3_marshalable_serialization_exceeds_max(v: Vec<u64>)
    requires
        v.is_marshalable(),
    ensures
        v.ghost_serialize().len() > usize::MAX,
{
}
reason: A marshalable vector's serialized size must fit in usize::MAX; exceeding it contradicts the size-bound invariant

name: huge_vec_always_marshalable
code:
proof fn phi_4_huge_vec_always_marshalable(v: Vec<u64>)
    requires
        v@.len() as int > usize::MAX / 8,
    ensures
        v.is_marshalable(),
{
}
reason: A Vec<u64> with length exceeding usize::MAX/8 would serialize to more than usize::MAX bytes; being marshalable would mean overflow is undetected

name: marshalable_implies_element_not_marshalable
code:
proof fn phi_5_marshalable_implies_element_not_marshalable(v: Vec<usize>, i: int)
    requires
        v.is_marshalable(),
        0 <= i < v@.len() as int,
    ensures
        !v@[i].is_marshalable(),
{
}
reason: If a vector is marshalable then every element must individually be marshalable; the negation would indicate the forall quantifier in the spec is inverted
